narrative:
Karnataka High Court heard 950 of 2,200 listed matters on 23 April, an overall hearing rate of **43.2%**. That is a clear improvement over **38.9%** on 22 April and **34.1%** on 21 April, even though the court handled fewer absolute matters than yesterday because the cause list itself was smaller. The day’s throughput was therefore stronger in conversion terms rather than in raw disposal volume.

On judge-wise performance, the strongest high-volume benches were **V. Srishananda** at **61 of 84 heard (72.6%)**, **E.S. Indiresh** at **101 of 152 (66.4%)**, **Anu Sivaraman** across three lists at **50 of 70 (71.4%)**, and **K. Manmadha Rao** at **41 of 67 (61.2%)**. **R. Nataraj** also converted well at **34 of 57 (59.6%)**. At the weaker end on meaningful dockets, **M. Nagaprasanna** heard **0 of 50**, one **Sachin Shankar Magadum** list was **0 of 85**, and **S. Rachaiah** had a sharp drop on one list at **1 of 25**, contributing to a modest combined day.

Stage-wise, the court moved best on **Orders** (**411 of 847, 48.5%**), followed by **Admission** (**47.0%**) and **Preliminary Hearing** (**45.2%**). Conversion fell notably at the more time-intensive end: **Final Hearing** reached only **78 of 285 (27.4%)** and **Further Hearing** **25 of 92 (27.2%)**. Even so, final-hearing conversion was slightly better than the **22.7%** seen on 22 April.

By case type, **WP** dominated the board with **976 listed** and **417 heard**, accounting for the largest share of both load and disposal; its **42.7%** conversion was materially better than **34.0%** yesterday. **MFA** remained efficient at **51.4%**, while **CRL.P** was a clear drag at **26.5%**, down from **41.4%** on 22 April. Smaller categories showed strong clearance where listed, especially **CMP (76.9%)**, **CRL.RP (74.1%)**, and **CCC (71.8%)**, while **STRP** lagged sharply at **6.2%**.
